title: Why I Am Not a Christian

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Assassination of James A. Garfield
memes.description: The assassination of U.S. President James A. Garfield by Charles Guiteau on July 2, 1881, which Robert G. Ingersoll analyzed as a case study in the dangers of religious fanaticism and sectarian belief.

memes.label: Bible
memes.description: The sacred text of Christianity and Judaism, subjected to extensive criticism by Robert G. Ingersoll, who argued against its divine inspiration and moral authority.

memes.label: Buddha
memes.description: Founder of Buddhism, discussed by Ingersoll in comparative religious context alongside Zoroaster, Confucius, and Christ.

memes.label: Chicago Anarchists Trial
memes.description: The trial and execution of the Chicago anarchists following the Haymarket affair of 1886, which Robert G. Ingersoll criticized as a miscarriage of justice driven by fear rather than evidence.

memes.label: Christianity
memes.description: The dominant religious tradition critiqued by Robert G. Ingersoll in his interviews and writings, characterized by belief in the Bible as divine revelation and in Jesus Christ as savior.

memes.label: Charles Darwin
memes.description: English naturalist and biologist (1809–1882), whose theory of evolution by natural selection transformed biology and challenged biblical literalism. Robert G. Ingersoll defended Darwin against religious attack and celebrated his work as a triumph of reason over dogma.

memes.label: Francis Bacon
memes.description: Francis Bacon (1561–1626), 1st Viscount St Alban, was an English philosopher, statesman, and pioneer of the scientific method. Ingersoll referenced Bacon in the context of the Shakespeare authorship controversy and as an example of how class prejudice shaped intellectual reputation.

memes.label: Freethought
memes.description: The epistemological stance that beliefs should be formed on the basis of reason, logic, and evidence rather than authority, tradition, or revelation — the intellectual tradition that Robert G. Ingersoll championed as "The Great Agnostic."

memes.label: God
memes.description: The supreme being of monotheistic religions, whose existence, nature, and moral character Robert G. Ingersoll subjected to sustained philosophical critique.

memes.label: Hell
memes.description: The doctrine of eternal conscious torment after death for the unrepentant — a belief Robert G. Ingersoll regarded as the single most morally indefensible element of orthodox Christianity, and the primary target of his theological critique.

memes.label: Henry Ward Beecher
memes.description: Henry Ward Beecher (1813–1887) was a prominent American clergyman, social reformer, and orator who engaged in public dialogue with Robert G. Ingersoll on matters of religion and morality.

memes.label: Alexander von Humboldt
memes.description: Alexander von Humboldt (1769–1859) was a Prussian naturalist, explorer, and polymath whose scientific work made him one of the most celebrated intellectuals of the 19th century. Ingersoll repeatedly invoked Humboldt as an exemplar of scientific achievement standing alongside Darwin.

memes.label: Immortality
memes.description: The doctrine that the human soul survives bodily death and persists eternally — a belief Robert G. Ingersoll examined critically, finding no evidence for it in either reason or revelation.

memes.label: Isaac Newton
memes.description: English mathematician and physicist whose religious views and character are discussed by Ingersoll in relation to scientific genius and Protestant orthodoxy.

memes.label: Jesus Christ
memes.description: Central figure of Christianity, discussed critically by Ingersoll in the context of religious belief and miracles.

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: Martin Luther (1483–1546) was a German theologian and the central figure of the Protestant Reformation. Ingersoll used Luther as a foil to demonstrate that the Reformation, while breaking from Rome, retained much of the intolerance and superstition of medieval Catholicism.

memes.label: Mohammed
memes.description: Founder of Islam, referenced by Ingersoll in discussions of religious morality, historical success, and the relationship between a teacher and their doctrines.

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: Biblical lawgiver and prophet whose historical existence and moral authority are critically examined by Ingersoll in "Mistakes of Moses."

memes.label: Paul the Apostle
memes.description: Early Christian apostle whose teachings on resurrection and doctrine are critically examined by Ingersoll in his assessment of Christian origins.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: The capacity for logical, rational, and analytic thought — the faculty by which humans apprehend truth through observation and experience rather than revelation or authority.

memes.label: The Resurrection of Jesus
memes.description: The Christian doctrine that Jesus of Nazareth rose bodily from the dead three days after his crucifixion — an event Robert G. Ingersoll examined forensically and found supported by no credible evidence, making it a central case study in his critique of faith-based belief.

memes.label: Robert G. Ingersoll
memes.description: Robert Green Ingersoll (1833–1899), known as "The Great Agnostic," was an American lawyer, orator, and freethinker who became the most prominent critic of organized religion in 19th-century America.

memes.label: William Shakespeare
memes.description: English playwright and poet (1564–1616), widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language. Robert G. Ingersoll considered Shakespeare the greatest human being who ever lived and used his works as a measure of unaided human genius against the claims of divine revelation.

memes.label: Slavery and the Bible
memes.description: The institution of chattel slavery as sanctioned in the Bible and practiced in the United States — a central topic in Robert G. Ingersoll's moral critique of scripture, demonstrating that the Bible, far from being a reliable moral guide, explicitly permitted and regulated human bondage.

memes.label: Superstition
memes.description: Belief or practice resulting from ignorance, fear of the unknown, or trust in supernatural causality without evidence — a category Robert G. Ingersoll used to distinguish genuine religion (rooted in compassion and inquiry) from the fear-based dogmas he critiqued.

memes.label: Thomas Paine
memes.description: Thomas Paine (1737–1809) was an English-American political activist, philosopher, and revolutionary. Ingersoll praised Paine as a champion of liberty and abolition whose memory deserved to stand alongside America's founders.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: That which conforms to fact or reality — in Ingersoll's philosophy, truth is discovered through reason and evidence, not revealed by authority or scripture, and no genuine truth can ever be harmful to humanity.

memes.label: Voltaire
memes.description: Voltaire (1694–1778), born François-Marie Arouet, was a French Enlightenment writer, philosopher, and satirist. Ingersoll invoked Voltaire as a champion of reason against religious authority, comparing his influence favorably against that of Martin Luther.
